start.title: A Peculiar Afternoon
start.text:
Theodore Ernest O'Hare -- Theo to everyone who knows him -- is seventeen, restless, and supposed to be reading a novel for English class. The copy of *The Great Gatsby* lies open on his desk, its pages soft and yellowed. Theo's eyelids are heavy. The afternoon light turns golden, then green -- an impossible, luminous green...

When Theo opens his eyes, the desk is gone. The book is gone. He is standing on a wooden train platform. A conductor in a peaked cap calls out: '*Last stop before the city! West Egg! East Egg!*' The air smells of salt water and gasoline. Automobiles gleam in the summer sun. A billboard across the tracks shows a pair of enormous eyes behind yellow spectacles, staring down at a gray wasteland of ash.

Theo checks his pockets. He's wearing a linen suit he doesn't own. In his breast pocket: a handwritten invitation to a party at the Gatsby mansion, tonight. In his trouser pocket: a train ticket with three stops remaining.

The conductor waits. '*Well, young man? Where to?*'

choices.text: Get off at West Egg -- follow the invitation to Gatsby's
Stay on to East Egg -- see how the old money lives
Get off here, in this strange ash-gray valley

vocabulary.definition: F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel about wealth, obsession, and the American Dream in the Jazz Age.
The 'new money' village on Long Island, home to self-made millionaires like Gatsby.
The 'old money' village, home to established families like the Buchanans.

act1_west_egg.title: The New Money Shore
act1_west_egg.text:
Theo steps off the train into West Egg. The houses here are enormous but eclectic -- a French chateau next to a Tudor mansion next to something that looks vaguely like a castle. Everything is new, bright, and slightly overdone.

A young man in a modest suit approaches. '*You look lost. I'm Nick -- Nick Carraway. I just moved here myself.*' He gestures at a small bungalow squeezed between two mansions. '*That's mine. The cottage cheese between two slices of marble cake.*'

Nick notices the invitation in Theo's hand. '*Gatsby's party? I got one too. Nobody actually knows who Gatsby is, you know. Some say he killed a man. Others say he was a German spy.*' He pauses. '*I live right next door to him. You can see his dock from my lawn. He stands out there at night, staring across the water at this green light.*'

From here, Theo can see the Gatsby mansion -- a colossal affair, a factual imitation of some Hotel de Ville in Normandy, with a marble swimming pool and forty acres of lawn. Music is already drifting from somewhere inside.

choices.text: Go to Gatsby's party with Nick tonight
Ask Nick about the green light across the water
Explore the West Egg waterfront alone first

vocabulary.definition: The novel's narrator, a Yale graduate from Minnesota who moves to West Egg and becomes Gatsby's neighbor and confidant.
French for 'new rich' -- people who have recently acquired wealth, often lacking the social graces of old money.

act1_east_egg.title: Old Money, Old Rules
act1_east_egg.text:
Theo rides the train one more stop to East Egg. The difference is immediate. Where West Egg was flashy and new, East Egg is restrained and ancient. The lawns are immaculate. The houses don't shout; they whisper.

A gleaming roadster pulls up beside him on the road. The driver is a large, powerful man with a cruel body and arrogant eyes. '*You're not from around here,*' he says. It isn't a question. '*I'm Tom Buchanan.*'

Tom's wife Daisy appears on the porch of their Georgian Colonial mansion, dressed in white, her voice low and thrilling -- the kind of voice that makes you lean toward it, that holds a promise that she'd done gay, exciting things just a while ago and that there were gay, exciting things hovering in the next hour. '*Do come in,*' she says. '*We're having the most absolutely terrible time of it. You'll be a wonderful distraction.*'

Inside, Theo meets Jordan Baker, a professional golfer, languid and bored, who watches everything with cool, skeptical eyes.

choices.text: Ask Tom about his views on civilization and society
Talk to Jordan Baker -- she seems to know secrets
Ask Daisy about the green light across the bay

vocabulary.definition: Daisy's husband, a former Yale football star from old money -- physically imposing, racist, and brutally careless.
Tom's wife and Gatsby's lost love, whose voice is 'full of money' -- beautiful, charming, and ultimately hollow.
Inherited wealth passed through generations, associated with established social status and unspoken codes of behavior.

act1_valley.title: The Valley of Ashes
act1_valley.text:
Theo steps off the train into a gray, desolate landscape. This is the **Valley of Ashes** -- a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat into ridges and hills and grotesque gardens, where ashes take the forms of houses and chimneys and rising smoke.

Above it all, the eyes of **Doctor T. J. Eckleburg** -- enormous, blue, retina-less -- stare down from a faded billboard. They are the eyes of God, or the eyes of nobody. They see everything and judge nothing. Or perhaps they judge everything and save nothing.

A gas station sits at the edge of the ash heaps. A spiritless man named George Wilson wipes his hands on a rag and stares at Theo without curiosity. '*Need gas? Nobody needs gas here. Nobody needs anything here.*'

From inside the station, a woman with tremendous vitality watches Theo with sharp, interested eyes. This is Myrtle Wilson, George's wife -- though everything about her strains against the life this valley has given her.

choices.text: Talk to George Wilson about life in the valley
Ask Myrtle about the world beyond the ashes
Study the eyes of Doctor Eckleburg -- what do they mean?

vocabulary.definition: The desolate industrial wasteland between West Egg and New York City, symbolizing the moral decay hidden by the wealth of the Eggs.
A faded billboard of an oculist's eyes that overlooks the Valley of Ashes, symbolizing the absence of God or moral oversight in the novel's world.

act1_green_light.title: The Light Across the Water
act1_green_light.text:
Nick leads Theo to the edge of his lawn. Across the dark water of the Sound, a single green light burns at the end of a dock. '*That's the Buchanan place,*' Nick says. '*East Egg.*'

As they watch, a figure emerges from the Gatsby mansion and walks to the edge of the dock. He stretches out his arms toward the green light, trembling. Then he vanishes back into the darkness.

'*That's him,*' Nick whispers. '*That's Gatsby.*'

Theo stares at the **green light**. It's just a dock light. Just a bulb at the end of a pier. But something about the way that figure reached for it -- with such yearning, such impossible hope -- makes Theo's chest ache.

Nick speaks softly. '*I think he's reaching for something. Something he lost a long time ago. Or something he never quite had.*'

choices.text: Go to Gatsby's party to learn who this mysterious man is
Cross the water to East Egg and find out what he's reaching for

vocabulary.definition: The light at the end of Daisy's dock, symbolizing Gatsby's longing for the past, the American Dream, and the unattainable future.

act1_waterfront.title: The Sound at Dusk
act1_waterfront.text:
Theo walks the West Egg waterfront alone. The mansions glow in the last light. Boats drift on Long Island Sound. The whole place feels like a stage set -- magnificent but somehow impermanent, as if a strong wind could blow it all away.

He passes a man sitting alone on a dock, staring at the water. The man is young, not much older than Theo, but dressed in a pink suit with a gold tie. He turns and smiles -- the most extraordinary smile Theo has ever seen. '*Hello, old sport,*' the man says. '*You must be the young fellow from the invitation. I'm Gatsby.*'

His smile is one of those rare smiles with a quality of eternal reassurance in it. It concentrates on you with an irresistible prejudice in your favor.

'*I throw these parties, you know,*' Gatsby says, '*but I confess I don't much enjoy them. I'm looking for someone. Someone I lost five years ago.*' His eyes drift across the water. '*Do you ever think the past can be repeated, old sport?*'

choices.text: 'Yes -- we can always go back to who we were'
'No -- you can't repeat the past'

vocabulary.definition: Born James Gatz, a self-made millionaire who reinvented himself to win back his lost love, Daisy Buchanan. His wealth comes from bootlegging.
Gatsby's signature phrase, used to affect an air of casual aristocratic familiarity he never naturally possessed.

act1_tom_views.title: The Rise of the Colored Empires
act1_tom_views.text:
Tom Buchanan leans forward, his massive frame tense with conviction. '*Have you read The Rise of the Colored Empires by this man Goddard?*' he demands. '*Well, it's a fine book, and everybody ought to read it. The idea is if we don't look out the white race will be -- will be utterly submerged.*'

Theo recognizes the type immediately: a man who confuses privilege with merit, inherited wealth with earned wisdom. Tom's racism isn't just personal prejudice -- it's the **social Darwinism** of a class that believes its position at the top is natural, inevitable, and threatened.

Daisy rolls her eyes. Jordan Baker examines her fingernails. This is clearly a performance they've all seen before.

'*Tom's always reading scientific books,*' Daisy says, her voice dripping with irony so delicate that Tom misses it entirely. '*They have very long words in them.*'

The phone rings. Tom excuses himself. Jordan leans over to Theo: '*You know who that is on the phone, don't you? He's got a woman in New York.*'

choices.text: Follow Tom to learn about his secret life
Stay and talk to Jordan about what she knows

vocabulary.definition: The misapplication of Darwin's evolution theory to human society, used to justify racial hierarchy and class inequality.
The defining trait of Tom and Daisy -- they smash up things and people, then retreat into their money and let other people clean up the mess.

act1_jordan.title: The Skeptical Observer
act1_jordan.text:
Jordan Baker reclines on a long white sofa, perfectly balanced, perfectly still. She is a **professional golfer** -- famous, Theo realizes, once he places the name. There was a scandal about her once, something about moving a ball in a tournament. She cheated, and got away with it.

'*You're staring,*' she says. '*Everyone stares. It's my cross to bear.*'

Jordan tells Theo a story. Five years ago, a young officer named Jay Gatsby was stationed near Louisville. He fell in love with Daisy Fay -- the most popular girl in town. They were inseparable for a month. Then Gatsby shipped overseas, and Daisy married Tom Buchanan and his string of polo ponies.

'*And now Gatsby has bought that enormous house across the bay,*' Jordan says, '*just so he can stare at the green light on her dock.*'

She fixes Theo with her cool, skeptical eyes. '*He's going to ask Nick to arrange a meeting. The question is whether anyone should help him. What Gatsby wants is impossible -- he wants to **repeat the past**.*'

choices.text: Go to Gatsby's party to see this obsession firsthand
Warn Gatsby that the past can't be repeated

vocabulary.definition: A professional golfer and Daisy's friend -- dishonest, independent, and coolly observant. She represents the new, liberated woman of the 1920s.
Gatsby's fundamental delusion -- his belief that he can recreate the love he shared with Daisy five years ago, as if time could be reversed.

act1_daisy_light.title: A Voice Full of Money
act1_daisy_light.text:
Daisy's face changes when Theo mentions the green light. For just a moment, something real flickers behind the charm -- something wistful and afraid.

'*Oh, that old light,*' she says, recovering quickly. '*It's just the dock light. It's been there for ages.*' But her hand trembles slightly as she lifts her glass.

Theo listens to Daisy talk. Her voice is extraordinary -- low, breathless, with a singing compulsion to it. Nick will later describe it perfectly: her voice is **full of money**. That's the inexhaustible charm in it, the jingle of it, the cymbals' song.

'*Do you want to know a terrible secret?*' Daisy whispers. '*When my daughter was born, I said, "I hope she'll be a fool -- that's the best thing a girl can be in this world, a beautiful little fool."*' She laughs, but it sounds like breaking glass.

Jordan appears in the doorway. '*There's a party across the bay tonight. At Gatsby's. Everyone goes.*'

choices.text: Go to Gatsby's party with Jordan
Stay and try to understand what Daisy is really saying

vocabulary.definition: Nick's famous description of Daisy's voice -- her charm is inseparable from her wealth and class. The allure is material, not spiritual.
Daisy's wish for her daughter, revealing her awareness that women in her world survive by not seeing too clearly.

act1_george.title: The Man in the Ashes
act1_george.text:
George Wilson is a blond, spiritless man, anemic and faintly handsome. His garage sits at the edge of the ash dumps, and everything about him -- his clothes, his skin, his hope -- is covered in a fine gray dust.

'*I've been here eleven years,*' George says, wiping the same wrench he's been wiping since Theo arrived. '*Myrtle and I came out from the city. I was going to make something of this place.*' He looks around the desolate station. '*I'm still going to.*'

He doesn't sound like he believes it. George Wilson is the **American Dream** in its most brutal form -- not the dream of Gatsby's lavish reinvention, but the dream of the ordinary man who works hard and gets nothing.

'*Tom Buchanan comes through here sometimes,*' George says. '*He promised to sell me a car. I keep asking, but he keeps not selling.*' He doesn't know that Tom comes to see Myrtle, not to sell cars.

The eyes of Doctor Eckleburg gaze down, seeing all, saying nothing.

choices.text: Head to West Egg -- the party invitation is burning a hole in your pocket
Ask George about the eyes on the billboard

vocabulary.definition: The belief that anyone in America can achieve success through hard work. Gatsby embodies its romantic extreme; George Wilson, its crushing failure.

act1_myrtle.title: Vitality in the Wasteland
act1_myrtle.text:
Myrtle Wilson is the opposite of her husband in every way. Where George is faded and passive, Myrtle burns with vitality. She carries her surplus flesh sensuously, and there is an immediately perceptible vitality about her as if the nerves of her body were continually smoldering.

'*I married him because I thought he was a gentleman,*' Myrtle tells Theo, glancing at George with contempt. '*I thought he knew something about breeding. But he wasn't fit to lick my shoe.*'

Tom Buchanan is her escape -- her ticket out of the ashes. She doesn't seem to realize that to Tom, she is a possession, not a person. He keeps her in a small apartment in New York, buys her a puppy, and breaks her nose when she says Daisy's name.

Myrtle represents the **class barrier** that the American Dream pretends doesn't exist. She can acquire Tom's things -- his clothes, his manners, his apartment -- but she can never acquire his world.

choices.text: Take the train to West Egg for Gatsby's party
Go to the New York apartment with Myrtle and Tom

vocabulary.definition: The invisible but rigid boundary between social classes that wealth alone cannot cross -- central to the novel's tragedy.
The nationwide ban on alcohol (1920-1933) that created massive opportunities for bootleggers like Gatsby and organized crime.

act1_eyes.title: The Eyes of God
act1_eyes.text:
Theo stands beneath the billboard and stares up. The eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg are blue and enormous -- their retinas are one yard high. They look out of no face, but instead from a pair of enormous yellow spectacles which pass over a nonexistent nose.

Evidently some wild wag of an oculist set them there to fatten his practice in the borough of Queens, and then sank down himself into eternal blindness, or forgot them and moved away. But his eyes, dimmed a little by many paintless days under sun and rain, brood on over the solemn dumping ground.

George Wilson, when his world finally collapses, will stare at these eyes and whisper: '*God sees everything.*' But Fitzgerald's point is the opposite -- there is no God watching over this valley. There is no moral authority. The eyes are an **empty symbol**, a billboard selling nothing to no one, gazing at a wasteland that the wealthy created and ignore.

A train whistle shrieks. There's still time to get to that party.

choices.text: Take the train to West Egg for Gatsby's party
Stay in the valley and witness the truth beneath the glamour

vocabulary.definition: A symbol that has lost its original meaning -- like Eckleburg's eyes, which were an advertisement but became the Valley's only 'witness.'
Fitzgerald's metaphor: the Valley of Ashes represents the moral consequences of the wealth and carelessness of East and West Egg.

act2_party.title: Gatsby's Party
act2_party.text:
The party is beyond anything Theo has imagined. The lights grow brighter as the earth lurches away from the sun, and now the orchestra is playing yellow cocktail music, and the opera of voices pitches a key higher. Laughter is easier minute by minute, spilled with prodigality, tipped out at a cheerful word.

In Gatsby's gardens, men and girls come and go like moths among the whisperings and the champagne and the stars. Trays of cocktails float through the crowd. The bar is stocked with gins and liquors and cordials -- all illegal under **Prohibition**, all flowing freely.

The guests trade rumors: '*He killed a man once.*' '*He was a German spy during the war.*' '*He's a bootlegger.*' '*He's an Oxford man.*' Nobody actually knows their host. This is the **Jazz Age** in miniature -- a spectacle of consumption where nobody knows who's paying or why.

Theo drifts through the crowd, dazzled.

choices.text: Find the library -- someone said there's a man examining Gatsby's books
Look for Gatsby himself -- the host nobody has met
Enjoy the spectacle -- dance, drink, lose yourself in it

vocabulary.definition: The 1920s cultural era defined by jazz music, economic prosperity, and social rebellion -- a term coined by Fitzgerald himself.
Someone who illegally manufactures or sells alcohol during Prohibition. Gatsby's fortune comes from bootlegging and other criminal enterprises.

act2_library.title: Owl Eyes and Real Books
act2_library.text:
In Gatsby's library, a stout, middle-aged man with enormous owl-eyed spectacles sits in astonishment, staring at the shelves. He has been drunk for about a week, he explains, and thought sobering up in the library might be interesting.

'*See!*' he cries. '*They're real! They're absolutely real! Pages and everything!*' He pulls a volume from the shelf and waves it. '*This fella's a regular Belasco. It's a triumph. What thoroughness! What realism! Knew when to stop, too -- didn't cut the pages.*'

Theo understands. **Owl Eyes** has discovered the essential truth about Gatsby's world: the books are real, but unread. The library is genuine, but it's a stage set. Everything in Gatsby's life is simultaneously authentic and performative. He bought real books because a fake would be too easy to detect -- but he never read them, because reading was never the point.

The performance *is* the reality. Gatsby has constructed an identity so thoroughly that the question of what's 'real' underneath becomes almost meaningless.

choices.text: Seek out Gatsby -- if the books are a performance, who is the performer?
Reflect on what it means to build an identity from scratch

vocabulary.definition: An unnamed party guest who discovers Gatsby's books are real but unread -- the only guest perceptive enough to see through the performance.
David Belasco, a famous theatrical producer known for elaborate, realistic stage sets. Owl Eyes compares Gatsby to him.

act2_meet_gatsby.title: The Most Extraordinary Smile
act2_meet_gatsby.text:
Theo finds himself talking to a man his own age who mentions he served in the Third Division during the war. '*I was in the Seventh Infantry,*' the man says. '*I was in the Argonne Forest.*'

Before Theo can respond, the man turns and smiles. It is one of those rare smiles with a quality of eternal reassurance in it, that you may come across four or five times in life. It faces -- or seems to face -- the whole external world for an instant, and then concentrates on you with an irresistible prejudice in your favor. It understands you just as far as you want to be understood, believes in you as you would like to believe in yourself.

'*I'm Gatsby,*' he says. '*I understand you've been looking for me.*'

He is elegant, formal, carefully composed. Every word seems rehearsed to perfection. And yet beneath the polish, Theo senses something desperate -- a man holding himself together by sheer will and an impossible dream.

choices.text: Ask Gatsby directly: 'Where are you really from?'
Ask about the woman across the bay
Accept the performance and enjoy the evening

vocabulary.definition: Gatsby's defining act: transforming himself from poor James Gatz into the wealthy, sophisticated Jay Gatsby through pure will and imagination.

act2_spectacle.title: Lost in the Music
act2_spectacle.text:
Theo gives in to the party. The champagne is cold. The music is hot. The Jazz Age swirls around him in a blur of laughter and silk and cigarette smoke. For one perfect evening, he understands the intoxication of it all -- the feeling that the world is limitless, that youth will last forever, that the party will never end.

But as the night wears on, Theo notices things. A woman crying in a corner. A man passed out in the garden. An argument that turns ugly near the swimming pool. Two girls in yellow are doing a dance routine, but their smiles look painted on.

By three in the morning, the glamour has curdled. Gatsby's party is a **spectacle of loneliness** -- hundreds of people drinking a stranger's liquor, dancing to a stranger's orchestra, going home to forget everything by morning.

In the driveway, a car has lost a wheel. Owl Eyes stumbles from the wreck. '*I wasn't even driving,*' he insists. The party is over.

choices.text: Find Gatsby alone after the party -- the mask must slip sometime
Leave West Egg. This world is hollow. Go home.

vocabulary.definition: Spending wealth publicly to display status -- Gatsby's parties are the supreme example, thrown not for pleasure but to attract Daisy's attention.
The decade of economic prosperity, cultural dynamism, and social change in America (1920-1929), ending with the stock market crash.

act2_apartment.title: The New York Apartment
act2_apartment.text:
Tom Buchanan keeps a small apartment on 158th Street for his affair with Myrtle Wilson. It is one large room, overcrowded with tapestried furniture too large for it. On the walls are photographs of Myrtle's mother, hovering like an ectoplasm on the wall.

Myrtle has changed her clothes and is now draped in an elaborate afternoon dress. She has changed her personality too -- her laughter, her gestures, her vitality have become violently affected, as if she is performing the role of a wealthy woman she's seen in magazines.

The party in the apartment grows drunken and ugly. Myrtle begins to chant Daisy's name. '*Daisy! Daisy! Daisy!*' Tom warns her to stop. She doesn't. With a short, deft movement, Tom Buchanan breaks her nose.

The violence is casual, practiced, the violence of a man who has never faced consequences. This is **Tom's world** -- one where wealth means you can break things and people without penalty.

choices.text: Leave -- get to Gatsby's party, anywhere but here
This violence reveals the truth about the rich. Investigate further.

vocabulary.definition: Tom's casual brutality toward Myrtle reflects the power imbalance in their affair -- she has no protection because their relationship is illicit.

act2_gatsby_truth.title: Can't Repeat the Past?
act2_gatsby_truth.text:
Gatsby stares at Theo, his composure cracking for just a moment. '*Can't repeat the past?*' he cries incredulously. '*Why of course you can!*'

He tells Theo his plan. Five years ago, he loved a girl named Daisy. She married another man while Gatsby was poor and overseas. So Gatsby made himself rich. He bought the mansion across the bay from her. He threw parties every weekend, hoping she would wander in. Every night he stands on his dock and reaches for the green light on hers.

'*I'm going to fix everything just the way it was before,*' Gatsby says. '*She'll see.*'

Theo realizes this is not just nostalgia. Gatsby has built an entire life -- a fortune, a mansion, an identity -- as an elaborate machine for **reversing time**. He doesn't want Daisy as she is now. He wants Daisy as she was at eighteen, in Louisville, before the war, before Tom, before everything.

This is magnificent. This is insane. This is the **American Dream** at its most beautiful and most deluded.

choices.text: Help Gatsby arrange the reunion with Daisy
Try to make Gatsby see reason before it's too late

vocabulary.definition: A sentimental longing for the past. Gatsby's nostalgia is extreme -- he doesn't just miss the past, he believes he can literally recreate it.

act2_daisy_depth.title: What Daisy Knows
act2_daisy_depth.text:
Alone with Theo, Daisy drops her charming mask for just a moment. She talks about her daughter, about her marriage, about the suffocating boredom of having everything and wanting nothing.

'*You see, I think everything's terrible anyhow,*' she says. '*And I know. I've been everywhere and seen everything and done everything.*' Her eyes fill with tears. '*Sophisticated -- God, I'm sophisticated!*'

Theo realizes Daisy sees clearly. She is not the fool she pretends to be. She knows Tom has affairs. She knows her life is empty. She knows the beautiful little fool routine is a survival strategy, not a philosophy.

But knowing doesn't change anything. Daisy's **paralysis** -- her inability to act on what she sees -- is the real tragedy. She has the intelligence to understand her prison and absolutely no will to escape it.

'*Shall we go to that party?*' she says brightly, tears already dried, mask already back in place. '*I hear it's marvelous.*'

choices.text: Go to Gatsby's party with Daisy
Skip the party -- find out about this Gatsby who's obsessed with her

vocabulary.definition: Daisy's defining quality -- the inability to act despite understanding. She sees the truth but cannot or will not change her circumstances.

act3_gatsby_past.title: James Gatz of North Dakota
act3_gatsby_past.text:
The truth about Gatsby comes out in fragments, like a broken mirror slowly reassembled. His name isn't Gatsby at all. He was born **James Gatz**, the son of shiftless, unsuccessful farm people in North Dakota. He invented 'Jay Gatsby' at seventeen -- the same age as Theo.

At seventeen, James Gatz saw a millionaire's yacht anchored on Lake Superior. The owner was Dan Cody, a copper magnate, who took the boy on as a personal assistant. For five years, Gatz sailed with Cody, learning the manners and vocabulary of wealth. When Cody died, he left Gatz $25,000. Cody's mistress cheated Gatz out of every penny.

So Gatsby learned two lessons: first, that wealth is the only language America respects. Second, that the wealthy will always find a way to take from you.

He reinvented himself completely. The accent, the 'old sport,' the Oxford story, the war medals -- every detail of Jay Gatsby is a **performance** of the self James Gatz wished he had been born as.

choices.text: Ask about his criminal connections -- where does the money really come from?
Understand the dream -- help arrange the reunion with Daisy

vocabulary.definition: Gatsby's birth name -- a poor boy from North Dakota who reinvented himself at seventeen into the sophisticated Jay Gatsby.
A wealthy copper magnate who mentored the young Gatz. Cody's wealth and lifestyle inspired Gatsby's transformation.
Fitzgerald's phrase for Gatsby's self-invention -- like Plato's ideal Forms, Gatsby created a perfect idea of who he should be, then willed it into reality.

act3_self_invention.title: The Platonic Conception
act3_self_invention.text:
Theo sits with Gatsby in his empty mansion after the last guests have left. The house feels enormous without the music and the crowds -- hollow, like a theater after the audience has gone.

'*Who are you, really?*' Theo asks.

Gatsby is quiet for a long time. Then he tells Theo about the boy from North Dakota who looked at a millionaire's yacht and decided to become someone else. He speaks about the **Platonic conception of himself** -- the ideal Jay Gatsby that James Gatz carries in his imagination, always slightly ahead of the real one, always beckoning forward.

'*The thing about it is,*' Gatsby says, '*I actually became him. I willed myself into existence. Isn't that the most American thing you ever heard?*'

Theo thinks about this. In America, you are supposed to be able to become anyone. Gatsby took that promise literally. He is the **American Dream made flesh** -- and the question Fitzgerald asks is whether that flesh is real or phantasmal.

choices.text: The dream is beautiful. Help Gatsby reach Daisy.
The dream is dangerous. Where does the money come from?

vocabulary.definition: An American ideal: the person who rises from nothing through talent and effort. Gatsby is both its triumph and its critique.

act3_wolfshiem.title: The Man Who Fixed the World Series
act3_wolfshiem.text:
Gatsby takes Theo to lunch in New York. At the restaurant, they meet Meyer **Wolfshiem**, a small, flat-nosed man with tiny eyes and enormous cuff links made of human molars.

Wolfshiem fixed the 1919 World Series. He rigged the outcome of the most sacred event in American sport. When Theo expresses shock, Wolfshiem merely blinks. '*I made him,*' Wolfshiem says, nodding toward Gatsby. '*I raised him up out of nothing, right out of the gutter. I saw right away he was a fine-appearing, gentlemanly young man.*'

So this is where Gatsby's money comes from: **organized crime**. Bootlegging, bond fraud, connections to men who fix baseball games and wear human teeth as jewelry. The beautiful mansion, the gorgeous shirts, the legendary parties -- all of it is built on criminality.

But Gatsby uses the money for only one purpose: to win back Daisy. The corruption is real, but so is the dream.

choices.text: The dream justifies the means. Help arrange the reunion.
The corruption taints everything. This will end badly.

vocabulary.definition: Gatsby's criminal associate, based on real-life gangster Arnold Rothstein, who actually fixed the 1919 World Series.
The 'Black Sox' scandal: eight Chicago White Sox players were bribed to lose the World Series. Wolfshiem's involvement shows the scale of corruption beneath the Twenties' surface.

act3_daisy_question.title: The Woman Across the Bay
act3_daisy_question.text:
Gatsby's composure cracks when Theo mentions the woman across the bay. His carefully maintained facade trembles, and for a moment, the real man shows through -- vulnerable, desperate, absurdly romantic.

'*Her name is Daisy,*' he says. '*We were in love, five years ago. I was a poor lieutenant, and she was the most popular girl in Louisville. I let her go because I had nothing to offer.*'

He gestures at the mansion, the grounds, the party still echoing in the distance. '*Now I have everything to offer. Everything I've done -- everything you see here -- it's all for her. She just doesn't know it yet.*'

Theo recognizes the scale of Gatsby's devotion. This isn't a man trying to rekindle a romance. This is a man who has organized his entire existence -- every dollar earned, every party thrown, every shirt purchased -- around a single, fixed point of light across the water.

choices.text: Help Gatsby -- arrange for him to meet Daisy again
This obsession seems dangerous. Learn more about Gatsby's past first.

vocabulary.definition: The act of perceiving someone as perfect, ignoring flaws. Gatsby has idealized Daisy for five years, building her into something no real person can match.

act4_reunion.title: The Shirts Scene
act4_reunion.text:
Theo helps arrange the reunion at Nick's cottage. Gatsby is terrified. He has Theo help him cut Nick's grass, fill the house with flowers, and arrange a catering spread that could feed twenty. For five years he has planned for this moment, and now that it's here, he nearly runs away.

Daisy arrives. There is an agonizing, awkward silence. Then Gatsby leads them to his mansion. Room by room, he shows Daisy his life -- the evidence of his transformation, his worth, his devotion.

In his bedroom, Gatsby opens his closets and begins throwing shirts -- shirts of sheer linen and thick silk and fine flannel -- piling them high on the bed in coral and apple-green and lavender and faint orange. Daisy bends her head into the shirts and begins to cry stormily. '*They're such beautiful shirts,*' she sobs. '*It makes me sad because I've never seen such -- such beautiful shirts before.*'

She is not crying about shirts. She is crying because Gatsby achieved the impossible, and because it is five years too late, and because she chose wrong, and because she can never unchoose.

choices.text: Believe in the dream -- maybe they can still make it work
See the tragedy forming -- this reunion will destroy them both

vocabulary.definition: The famous moment when Gatsby throws his expensive shirts for Daisy, who weeps -- the shirts symbolize the material excess that both attracts and repels her.
The overwhelming accumulation of luxury goods, central to Gatsby's strategy of proving his worth through possessions.

act4_green_light_scene.title: The Colossal Significance of That Light
act4_green_light_scene.text:
Gatsby stands with Daisy at the end of his dock. He points across the water at the green light. '*You always have a green light that burns all night at the end of your dock,*' he tells her.

Daisy puts her arm through his, and Gatsby seems to realize something devastating. Now that Daisy is here, beside him, the **green light** is just a green light again. The colossal significance of that enchanted object has vanished forever. The dream has been made real, and in becoming real, it has lost the thing that made it beautiful.

This is the **paradox of the American Dream**: it is most powerful as aspiration. Once achieved, it can only disappoint, because no reality can match the perfection of the imagined future.

Gatsby doesn't see this yet. He believes the magic is just beginning. But Theo, watching from the shadows, understands: the count of enchanted objects has diminished by one.

choices.text: Stay with Gatsby as the dream unravels -- see it through to the end
Leave now, while the beauty still holds -- some stories are better unfinished

vocabulary.definition: An ordinary thing invested with extraordinary meaning by desire. The green light was enchanted by Gatsby's longing; Daisy's presence breaks the spell.
The Dream is most beautiful when it remains a dream. Achievement kills the aspiration that gave it power.

act5_plaza.title: The Hottest Day
act5_plaza.text:
It is the hottest day of summer, and everything comes apart at the Plaza Hotel. Tom, Daisy, Gatsby, Jordan, Nick, and Theo are crowded into a suite, the heat pressing in, tempers fraying.

Tom finally confronts Gatsby. '*I found out what your "drug-stores" were,*' he says, his voice thick with contempt. '*You're nothing but a **bootlegger**.*'

Gatsby turns to Daisy. This is the moment. '*Daisy, tell him. Tell him you never loved him. Tell him you've always loved me.*'

Daisy tries. '*I never loved him,*' she says -- but her voice wavers. '*I loved you too,*' she whispers to Tom. Gatsby's face is stricken. He has asked Daisy to erase five years of her life, to say that her marriage, her daughter, her entire adult existence meant nothing. Even Daisy cannot sustain that lie.

Tom presses his advantage. He exposes Gatsby's criminal past. Daisy retreats into Tom's world of old money and familiar cruelty. The dream is dying in the heat of a hotel room.

choices.text: Follow Gatsby and Daisy as they drive home -- something terrible is about to happen
Stay at the Plaza and reflect on what the confrontation reveals

vocabulary.definition: The iconic New York hotel where the novel's climactic confrontation occurs -- wealth's stage for its own destruction.
The novel's central class conflict: Tom's inherited wealth (legitimized by time) vs. Gatsby's earned wealth (tainted by its origins).

act5_accident.title: Death in the Valley of Ashes
act5_accident.text:
Gatsby drives home with Daisy in his yellow car. They are going too fast through the Valley of Ashes when Myrtle Wilson -- Tom's mistress, desperate, running toward a car she thinks is Tom's -- dashes into the road.

The car strikes her. Myrtle Wilson is killed instantly. Her left breast swings loose like a flap, and her mouth is wide open and ripped at the corners. The violence of it is obscene.

Gatsby tells Theo the truth: **Daisy was driving**. Daisy killed Myrtle, and Gatsby is going to take the blame. '*She was very nervous and she thought it would steady her to drive,*' he says. He stands outside the Buchanan house all night, watching the windows, ready to protect Daisy from Tom.

But inside, Tom and Daisy are sitting at the kitchen table. They aren't happy, and they aren't unhappy. They are conspiring. They are retreating into the fortress of their money, leaving Gatsby to face the consequences of Daisy's carelessness.

choices.text: Warn Gatsby -- he's in danger and Daisy won't save him
Confront Daisy -- she has to take responsibility

vocabulary.definition: Daisy's killing of Myrtle and failure to stop -- the ultimate act of carelessness, the defining sin of the wealthy in Fitzgerald's vision.
Gatsby willingly becomes the scapegoat for Daisy's crime, sacrificing himself for a woman who will not sacrifice anything for him.

act5_confrontation_meaning.title: The Architecture of Collapse
act5_confrontation_meaning.text:
In the silence after everyone leaves the Plaza, Theo sits and thinks about what just happened. The confrontation revealed the novel's deepest truth: **class is destiny in America**, no matter what the Dream promises.

Gatsby did everything right. He got rich. He got powerful. He got the mansion and the parties and the shirts. But when Tom Buchanan says '*He's a bootlegger,*' it's over. Not because bootlegging is worse than Tom's racism, violence, or adultery -- it isn't. But because Tom's sins are protected by old money's invisible armor, and Gatsby's are not.

Daisy chose Tom not because she loves him but because Tom is **safe**. Tom's world, cruel as it is, will never collapse. Gatsby's world, magnificent as it is, was always one revelation away from ruin.

The **social stratification** of East Egg and West Egg isn't just geography. It's an unbridgeable moral chasm.

choices.text: Rush after Gatsby and Daisy -- something terrible is happening on the road
This understanding is enough. It's time to go home.

vocabulary.definition: The hierarchical arrangement of social classes. In Gatsby, old money (East Egg) sits permanently above new money (West Egg), which sits above the Valley of Ashes.

act6_vigil.title: Gatsby's Vigil
act6_vigil.text:
Theo finds Gatsby standing outside the Buchanan house at dawn, still watching the windows. He has stood there all night. He is waiting for Daisy to turn off her light, to signal that she is safe, that Tom hasn't hurt her.

'*She'll come to me,*' Gatsby says, but his voice has lost its certainty.

Theo tries to warn him. George Wilson knows the yellow car. George thinks the car's owner killed his wife. Tom will tell George it was Gatsby's car. Gatsby is in danger.

But Gatsby won't leave. He cannot abandon the dream, even now, even when the dream has clearly abandoned him. He is like a man drowning who refuses to let go of a golden anchor.

'*I'm going to drain the pool,*' Gatsby says. '*I haven't used it all summer.*' It is a strange, sad detail. All summer, throwing parties for hundreds of people, and he never once swam in his own pool.

choices.text: Stay with Gatsby -- don't let him face this alone
Run to George Wilson's garage -- stop what's coming

vocabulary.definition: When the audience knows something a character doesn't. Gatsby waits for Daisy, not knowing she has already chosen Tom and will never come.

act6_careless.title: Careless People
act6_careless.text:
Theo storms into the Buchanan mansion, determined to confront Daisy. He finds her and Tom at the kitchen table, eating cold fried chicken, their heads close together in quiet conspiracy.

'*You killed her,*' Theo says to Daisy. '*You were driving. Gatsby is going to take the blame for you, and you're eating chicken.*'

Daisy's eyes widen, but Tom's hand closes over hers. '*We're leaving,*' Tom says. '*We're going away for a while.*' And Theo realizes they've already decided. They will leave. They will let Gatsby take the fall. They will let George Wilson's grief find its target.

Nick will later describe them perfectly: '*They were **careless people**, Tom and Daisy -- they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness, or whatever it was that kept them together, and let other people clean up the mess they had made.*'

choices.text: Race back to Gatsby -- warn him, save him if you can
Go to George Wilson -- tell him the truth before he does something terrible

vocabulary.definition: Nick's devastating verdict on Tom and Daisy: they destroy lives without consequence, shielded by wealth and privilege from ever facing accountability.

act6_pool.title: The Pool
act6_pool.text:
Theo races back to Gatsby's mansion. He finds Gatsby floating on an air mattress in the pool, staring up at the sky. The phone rings inside the house. Gatsby's butler goes to answer it.

For one suspended moment, everything is still. The leaves are falling from the trees. Summer is over. Gatsby floats in the pool he never used, in the mansion built for a woman who never came, surrounded by a fortune assembled for a dream that was already dead.

George Wilson appears at the edge of the garden, a revolver in his hand. Tom told him it was Gatsby's car.

Theo shouts a warning. Gatsby turns. The phone is ringing -- perhaps it's Daisy, calling at last. We'll never know.

choices.text: Stay for the funeral -- see how the world honors Jay Gatsby
Theo has seen enough. It's time to understand what it all means.

vocabulary.definition: A noble figure brought down by a fatal flaw. Gatsby's flaw is his magnificent, impossible faith that the future could restore the past.

act6_wilson.title: The Eyes Saw Everything
act6_wilson.text:
Theo runs to the Valley of Ashes, to George Wilson's garage. He finds George in a state of wild grief, staring at the eyes of Doctor Eckleburg through the window.

'*God sees everything,*' George whispers, pointing at the billboard.

Theo tries to tell George the truth -- that it wasn't Gatsby driving, that Tom Buchanan's mistress was killed by Tom's world, not Gatsby's. But George is beyond reason. His grief has curdled into something simple and lethal.

Tom Buchanan has already been here. Tom told George that Gatsby owned the yellow car. Tom used George's grief as a weapon, pointing it at the one man who threatens Tom's hold on Daisy. It is the **cruelest act in the novel** -- casual, deliberate, and utterly without consequence for Tom.

George leaves the garage with a revolver. Theo cannot stop him.

choices.text: Race to Gatsby's mansion -- there might still be time
Stay in the valley. The eyes of Eckleburg stare down. What does it all mean?

vocabulary.definition: When punishment fits the crime in a story. Fitzgerald denies poetic justice: the guilty go unpunished, and the innocent (Gatsby, George, Myrtle) are destroyed.

act6_funeral.title: Nobody Came
act6_funeral.text:
Gatsby's funeral is attended by three people: Nick, Theo, and Owl Eyes -- the man from the library who marveled that the books were real.

Three people. Not the hundreds who drank Gatsby's champagne. Not the celebrities who danced in his garden. Not Daisy, who never even sends a flower. Not Wolfshiem, who '*raised him up out of nothing.*'

Owl Eyes takes off his glasses and wipes them. '*The poor son of a bitch,*' he says.

The rain falls. The small group stands around the grave of Jay Gatsby, who was born James Gatz, who dreamed bigger than anyone, who reached further than anyone, and who died alone in a pool he never swam in, murdered by a man who didn't know him, for a crime committed by a woman who didn't love him.

Gatsby's father arrives late, clutching a photograph of the mansion. He is proud of his son. He never understood what any of it was for.

choices.text: Reflect on what Gatsby's life and death mean for the American Dream
Leave. Go back to the Midwest, where people are honest.

vocabulary.definition: Gatsby surrounded himself with people but died utterly alone. His funeral reveals that his connections were all transactional, not genuine.

end_borne_back.title: Borne Back Ceaselessly
end_borne_back.text:
On his last night before leaving, Theo walks down to the beach. The moon is bright. The mansions of West Egg glow against the dark water. Across the Sound, the green light burns on Daisy's dock.

And Theo thinks about the Dutch sailors who first saw this island -- a fresh, green breast of the new world. For a transitory enchanted moment, man must have held his breath in the presence of this continent, face to face for the last time in history with something commensurate to his capacity for wonder.

Gatsby believed in the green light, the **orgastic future** that year by year recedes before us. It eluded us then, but that's no matter -- tomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms farther...

And one fine morning --

So we beat on, boats against the current, **borne back ceaselessly into the past**.

Theo wakes at his desk. The book lies open to the last page. His eyes are wet. He understands now: the green light is not about Gatsby. It's about all of us -- reaching for a future that is always, secretly, a version of the past we've already lost. And we reach anyway, because that is what it means to be human.

vocabulary.definition: Fitzgerald's coined term (often misprinted as 'orgiastic') for the ecstatic, shimmering future we believe is ahead of us but that always recedes.

vocabulary.term: borne back ceaselessly into the past
vocabulary.definition: The novel's final line: no matter how hard we strain forward, the current of time pulls us backward. The American Dream is beautiful because it is impossible.

end_understanding.title: The Fundamental Decencies
end_understanding.text:
Theo leaves before the end. He has seen enough: the green light, the parties, the dream, the rot beneath the glamour. He doesn't need to see Gatsby die to understand what Fitzgerald is saying.

Walking to the train station, Theo passes through the Valley of Ashes one last time. The eyes of Doctor Eckleburg stare down. George Wilson sweeps his garage. Myrtle looks out the window with hungry eyes.

Nick once said that after returning from the East, he wanted the world to be in uniform and at a sort of **moral attention** forever. Theo understands this now. The novel is not anti-wealth or anti-ambition. It is anti-**carelessness** -- the carelessness of people who can afford to break things and walk away.

Theo wakes at his desk. The Gatsby novel lies open. He picks it up and reads the last page, knowing now what every word costs. The American Dream is not the problem. The problem is who gets destroyed when dreamers are careless with other people's lives.

vocabulary.definition: Nick's phrase for the ethical vigilance he craves after witnessing the Buchanans' destructiveness -- a world where people are held accountable.

end_midwest.title: Back Where People Are Honest
end_midwest.text:
Theo decides he's had enough of the East. Like Nick Carraway, he wants to go back to the Midwest, where the values are simpler and people look you in the eye.

But even as he thinks this, Theo recognizes the irony. The Midwest isn't really more honest than the East. Nick isn't really more moral than Jordan or Tom. The desire to retreat to an innocent past is exactly Gatsby's mistake -- the belief that somewhere behind you is a pure place you can return to.

Still, Theo understands something important: you don't have to stay in a corrupt world just because the alternative is imperfect. Sometimes wisdom is knowing when to leave.

Theo wakes at his desk. The novel lies open. He understands the story but wonders if he has truly reckoned with its darkest implications -- or if, like Nick, he has simply retreated into comfortable judgment.

vocabulary.definition: Nick's return to the Midwest mirrors his retreat from moral complexity -- understanding without fully engaging.

end_eckleburg.title: The Eyes That See Everything
end_eckleburg.text:
Theo stands in the Valley of Ashes, unable to look away from the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg. George Wilson is gone, off to commit an act of desperate, misdirected justice. The ashes settle.

Fitzgerald placed these eyes at the center of his geography for a reason. Between the new money of West Egg and the old money of East Egg lies this wasteland -- the **cost** of their wealth, the people ground into ash by the machinery of American prosperity.

The eyes see everything but do nothing. There is no God in this novel, no moral authority, no cosmic justice. Myrtle is killed by the woman whose husband she was sleeping with. Gatsby is killed by the man whose wife he never touched. Tom and Daisy retreat into their money and face no consequences at all.

Theo wakes at his desk. He looks at the novel with new eyes. The Great Gatsby is not a love story. It is an autopsy of a country that promises everything and accounts for nothing.

vocabulary.definition: The absence of any system of justice or accountability in the novel's world -- the eyes watch, but no one acts.

end_party.title: Just a Party
end_party.text:
Theo leaves the party at dawn, ears ringing, head swimming. It was magnificent. It was the most extraordinary night of his life. And he doesn't have the slightest idea what any of it meant.

He saw the lights and the dancing and the champagne. He heard the rumors about Gatsby. He watched the owl-eyed man marvel at the books. But he never looked beneath the surface. He never asked why a man would throw parties for strangers, or what the green light meant, or why the Valley of Ashes sits between two kingdoms of wealth.

Theo was, in the end, just another party guest -- one of the hundreds who came, drank, and left without ever meeting their host or understanding his purpose.

Theo wakes at his desk. He finishes the novel dutifully, but the words slide past him. He got a B+ on the exam. He's pretty sure it was about the American Dream or something.

end_unfinished.title: The Unfinished Dream
end_unfinished.text:
Theo walks away from Gatsby's dock while the green light still burns. He chooses to leave while the dream is still beautiful, before the Plaza confrontation, before the yellow car in the valley, before the empty funeral.

It is a kind of mercy -- and a kind of cowardice. Theo spares himself the novel's devastating final act. But in doing so, he misses its deepest truth: that the beauty of the dream is inseparable from its destruction. You cannot understand the green light without understanding what it costs.

Fitzgerald wrote: '*No amount of fire or freshness can challenge what a man will store up in his ghostly heart.*' Theo chose not to see what was stored in Gatsby's.

Theo wakes at his desk. He remembers the party, the smile, the green light. He thinks the novel is about hope. He's not wrong. But he's not right enough.

vocabulary.definition: Fitzgerald's phrase for the inner life where we store our deepest longings -- the private, invisible core of human desire.
